file_name: Han et al. - 2020 - Assessment of deep neural networks for the diagnosis of benign and malignant skin neoplasms in compa.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used a convolutional neural network (CNN) algorithm (specifically a region-based CNN or RCNN) to diagnose benign and malignant skin neoplasms from clinical photographs. The AI model's performance was evaluated and compared against dermatologists in both real-world and experimental settings.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' and a 'retrospective validation study'. It presents original experimental results, including a retrospective analysis of 40,331 clinical images from 10,426 cases, comparing the diagnostic performance of a convolutional neural network (CNN) algorithm against 65 attending physicians and 44 dermatologists. The paper includes specific methods, results with statistical metrics (AUC, sensitivity, specificity, p-values), and conclusions derived from new data, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Assessment of deep neural networks for the diagnosis of benign and malignant skin neoplasms in comparison with dermatologists: A retrospective validation study
brief_summary: This retrospective validation study assessed the diagnostic performance of a convolutional neural network (CNN) algorithm for distinguishing benign from malignant skin neoplasms using clinical photographs. The algorithm was evaluated on a large external dataset of 40,331 images from 10,426 cases obtained from Severance Hospital in Seoul, Korea. Its performance was compared against 65 attending physicians in real-world practice and 44 dermatologists in experimental settings where only images were provided. The algorithm achieved an area under the curve (AUC) of 0.863 for malignancy detection, with sensitivity and specificity comparable to those of dermatologists in the experimental reader test. However, its performance was inferior to that of attending physicians who utilized clinical information alongside visual findings. The study concludes that while the AI model matches dermatologist accuracy using images alone, integrating clinical metadata could further improve diagnostic precision.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnosis of malignancy (benign vs. malignant) and multiclass diagnosis of specific skin neoplasm types.
all_task_types.details: The study employs a convolutional neural network (CNN) for two classification tasks: (1) Binary classification to distinguish malignant from benign skin neoplasms using a calculated 'malignancy output' score derived from class probabilities; (2) Multiclass classification to predict the exact diagnosis among 32 specific disorder classes. The model outputs discrete diagnostic categories (e.g., basal cell carcinoma, seborrheic keratosis) and binary labels (malignant/benign).

datasets_used.name: Severance Dataset A
datasets_used.description: A retrospective validation dataset obtained from the Department of Dermatology, Severance Hospital, Seoul, Korea, covering the period from January 1, 2008 to March 31, 2019. It includes all single-lesion biopsied cases from patients over 19 years of age with pathologic diagnosis of one of 43 primary skin neoplasms.
classes_used: Malignant skin neoplasms (1,222 cases): Basal cell carcinoma, Squamous cell carcinoma, Intraepithelial carcinoma (SCC in situ), Keratoacanthoma, Malignant melanoma, Angiosarcoma, Dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans, Kaposi sarcoma, Merkel cell carcinoma

Benign skin neoplasms (9,204 cases): Actinic keratosis, Angiofibroma, Angiokeratoma, Becker nevus, Blue nevus, Cafe au lait macule, Congenital nevus, Dermatofibroma, Epidermal cyst, Epidermal nevus, Hemangioma, Juvenile xanthogranuloma, Lentigo, Lymphangioma, Melanocytic nevus, Milia, Mucocele, Mucosal melanotic macule, Neurofibroma, Nevus spilus, Organoid nevus, Ota nevus, Porokeratosis, Poroma, Port wine stain, Pyogenic granuloma, Sebaceous hyperplasia, Seborrheic keratosis, Skin tag, Spitz nevus, Syringoma, Venous lake, Wart, Xanthelasma

use.training: Not used for training; the algorithm was trained on external data (Asan Medical Center and web images).
use.validation: Used for external validation of the algorithm's generalizability.
use.testing: Used for binary classification testing (unprocessed images) and comparison with 65 attending physicians in real-world practice.
use.other: Served as the source for Dataset B and Dataset C.

datasets_used.name: Severance Dataset B
datasets_used.description: A subset of Severance Dataset A, compiled by excluding cases belonging to small classes (<10 cases) and untrained classes. It contains 32 specific disorder classes used for multiclass classification analysis.
classes_used: Malignant skin neoplasms (1,154 cases): Basal cell carcinoma, Squamous cell carcinoma, Intraepithelial carcinoma (SCC in situ), Keratoacanthoma, Malignant melanoma

Benign skin neoplasms (9,161 cases): Actinic keratosis, Angiokeratoma, Becker nevus, Blue nevus, Congenital nevus, Dermatofibroma, Epidermal cyst, Epidermal nevus, Hemangioma, Lentigo, Lymphangioma, Melanocytic nevus, Mucocele, Mucosal melanotic macule, Neurofibroma, Organoid nevus, Ota nevus, Porokeratosis, Poroma, Port wine stain, Pyogenic granuloma, Seborrheic keratosis, Skin tag, Syringoma, Venous lake, Wart, Xanthelasma

use.testing: Used for multiclass classification testing (cropped images) and binary classification testing (cropped images).
use.other: Served as the source for Dataset C.

datasets_used.name: Severance Dataset C
datasets_used.description: A subset of Severance Dataset B consisting of randomly selected cases used for the reader test. It contains 31 disorders (excluding Keratoacanthoma which had few cases in this subset or was excluded from this specific reader test configuration in some contexts, though Table 1 lists 31 disorders for C).
classes_used: Malignant skin neoplasms (152 cases): Basal cell carcinoma, Intraepithelial carcinoma (SCC in situ), Malignant melanoma, Squamous cell carcinoma

Benign skin neoplasms (1,168 cases): Actinic keratosis, Angiokeratoma, Becker nevus, Blue nevus, Congenital nevus, Dermatofibroma, Epidermal cyst, Epidermal nevus, Hemangioma, Lentigo, Lymphangioma, Melanocytic nevus, Mucocele, Mucosal melanotic macule, Neurofibroma, Organoid nevus, Ota nevus, Porokeratosis, Poroma, Port wine stain, Pyogenic granuloma, Seborrheic keratosis, Skin tag, Syringoma, Venous lake, Wart, Xanthelasma

use.other: Used for multiclass classification testing in the reader test setting.

datasets_used.description: An external public dataset used for multiclass classification testing. It contains 10 disorders and 1,300 images.
classes_used: 10 disorders (specific classes not listed in the provided text, but referenced as '10 disorders')

use.testing: Used for multiclass classification testing (cropped images) to assess generalizability.

datasets_used.name: Algorithm Training Dataset
datasets_used.description: The dataset used to train the skin cancer detection algorithm (RCNN). It comprised clinical images from Asan Medical Center and images gathered from websites, totaling 1,106,886 cropped images involving 178 disease classes.
classes_used: 178 disease classes (specific classes not listed in the provided text)

use.training: Used to train the region-based CNN and disease classifier (SENet/SE-ResNeXt-50).
use.other: Source of algorithm weights used in the study.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the diagnostic performance for binary classification of malignancy and multiclass classification of specific disorders.
To quantify the overall diagnostic accuracy of the algorithm and clinical diagnoses.
To compute the 95% confidence intervals (CI) for AUC values.
To compare sensitivity and specificity between the algorithm and clinical diagnoses (attending physicians) in real-world practice settings.
To compare sensitivity, specificity, and Top-n accuracies between the algorithm and dermatologists in the reader test setting.
To compare AUC values between different groups or methods.
To estimate the uncertainty for sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value (PPV), negative predictive value (NPV), and AUC.
To determine statistical significance for all comparisons.

hypothesis_testing.test: McNemar test, Wilcoxon signed-rank test, DeLong test
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in diagnostic performance (sensitivity, specificity, or AUC) between the deep neural network algorithm and the clinical diagnoses (attending physicians or dermatologists).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in diagnostic performance (sensitivity, specificity, or AUC) between the deep neural network algorithm and the clinical diagnoses.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: A p-value of < 0.05 was considered to indicate a statistically significant difference. The algorithm's performance was significantly inferior to attending physicians in real-world practice (McNemar test; p < 0.0001). In the reader test, the algorithm's performance was comparable to dermatologists for sensitivity and specificity (Wilcoxon test; p > 0.05), but the algorithm's Top-1/Top-3 accuracies were significantly superior to participants (Wilcoxon test; p < 0.0001) and inferior to clinical diagnoses (Wilcoxon test; p < 0.0001). Differences in AUC between algorithm and dermatologists were not statistically significant (DeLong test; p > 0.05).

Severance Dataset A.description: External validation dataset; 10,426 cases (1,222 malignant, 9,204 benign); unprocessed images used for binary classification.
Severance Dataset A.notes: The algorithm's performance at the high-specificity threshold was inferior to the first clinical impression of attending physicians (McNemar test; p < 0.0001). The algorithm's performance at the high-sensitivity threshold was inferior to the Top-3 clinical diagnosis (McNemar test; p < 0.0001).

Severance Dataset B.description: Subset of Dataset A excluding small/untrained classes; 10,315 cases; 32 disorders; cropped images used for multiclass and binary classification.
Severance Dataset B.notes: The algorithm's Top-1 and Top-3 accuracies were inferior to those of the clinical diagnoses (Wilcoxon test; p < 0.0001).

Edinburgh Dermofit Image Library.description: External public dataset; 1,300 images; 10 disorders; cropped images used for multiclass classification.

Severance Dataset C.description: Subset of Dataset B; 1,320 cases; 31 disorders (Keratoacanthoma excluded from reader test configuration in some contexts, but 32 choices provided); used for reader test with 44 dermatologists.
Severance Dataset C.notes: The difference in sensitivity between the algorithm (high-sensitivity threshold) and participants (Top-3) was not statistically significant (Wilcoxon test; p = 0.985). The difference in specificity between the algorithm (high-sensitivity threshold) and participants (Top-3) was statistically significant (Wilcoxon test; p = 0.004). The difference in specificity between the algorithm (high-specificity threshold) and participants (Top-1) was not statistically significant (Wilcoxon test; p = 0.097).

Severance Dataset C (Multiclass).description: Same as above; used for multiclass classification comparison.
Severance Dataset C (Multiclass).notes: The algorithm's Top-1 and Top-3 accuracies were superior to those of the participants (Wilcoxon test; p < 0.0001) and inferior to those of the clinical diagnoses (Wilcoxon test; p < 0.0001).

Cropped-Image Analysis (Severance Datase.description: Comparison of unprocessed vs cropped image analysis performance.
Cropped-Image Analysis (Severance Datase.notes: The AUC of cropped-image analysis was slightly better than unprocessed-image analysis (DeLong test; p = 0.002).

Robustness Analysis (Severance Dataset A.description: Assessment of performance with added impossible cases, inadequate quality images, and excluded classes.
Robustness Analysis (Severance Dataset A.notes: The difference between the AUCs of the black (Dataset A) and blue (Dataset A + impossible/inadequate) curves was not statistically significant (DeLong test; p = 0.746). The difference between the AUCs of the black and red (Dataset A - small/untrained) curves was not statistically significant (DeLong test; p = 0.428).

architecture_used.main_model: Region-based Convolutional Neural Network (RCNN) with a disease classifier using SENet/SE-ResNeXt-50 backbone
architecture_used.pretraining: Trained on a dataset of 1,106,886 cropped images involving 178 disease classes from Asan Medical Center and web images; used without modification for this study
architecture_used.input_size: Unprocessed clinical photographs (blob detection applied) or cropped images around the lesion of interest

architecture_details.output_type: Probable diagnoses with numerical probability values (0.0 to 1.0) and malignancy output (0 to 100)
architecture_details.malignancy_output_formula: malignancy output = (basal cell carcinoma output + squamous cell carcinoma output + intraepithelial carcinoma output + keratoacanthoma output + malignant melanoma output) + 0.2 × (actinic keratosis output + ulcer output)

training_details.training_dataset: 1,106,886 cropped images from 178 disease classes (Asan Medical Center and web images)
training_details.thresholds: Predefined high-sensitivity and high-specificity thresholds defined in previous study [18]

classification_or_output_head.binary_conversion: Malignancy output calculated from class probabilities for binary classification of benign vs. malignant

preprocessing_techniques: Blob detection for lesion localization in unprocessed images
Fine image selector to mark blobs containing possible skin lesions
Cropping of images around the lesion of interest (cropped-image analysis)
Exclusion of cases belonging to small classes (<10 cases) and untrained classes from Dataset B
Random sampling and shuffling of image sets for the reader test

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Synthetic data generation via Region-Based CNN
data_augmentation_techniques.details: The training dataset included images generated by a region-based CNN to maximize explainability and build the dataset efficiently.
The algorithm utilized cropped images around the lesion of interest for analysis, in addition to unprocessed clinical photographs.
Severance Dataset C was composed of 1,320 randomly selected cases from Severance Dataset B for the reader test.

conclusions: The convolutional neural network algorithm diagnosed skin tumors with nearly the same accuracy as dermatologists when the diagnosis was performed solely based on clinical photographs.
The algorithm's performance was inferior to that of attending physicians in real-world practice who utilized clinical information alongside visual findings.
The study's generalizability was limited by the exclusive use of high-quality clinical photographs taken in hospitals.
The study population lacked ethnic diversity.
To achieve more accurate predictive diagnoses, clinical information should be integrated with imaging information.
important_remarks: The study population lacked ethnic diversity, being exclusively from a single hospital in Seoul, Korea, which limits generalizability to other populations.

The algorithm's performance was inferior to attending physicians in real-world practice (AUC 0.863 vs clinical diagnosis superiority, p < 0.0001) because it lacked access to clinical metadata (e.g., patient history) that physicians utilized.

The algorithm was trained on 1,106,886 images from Asan Medical Center and web sources, but the external validation dataset (Severance Hospital) was completely independent, demonstrating generalizability across different hospital archives.

The study included 43 disorders, but several classes (e.g., angiosarcoma, dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans, Kaposi sarcoma, Merkel cell carcinoma, Spitz nevus) were present in the training/validation data but excluded from the multiclass analysis due to small sample sizes (<10 cases) or being 'untrained' in the specific multiclass model, representing a limitation in coverage.

The algorithm performed comparably to 44 dermatologists in an experimental setting where only images were provided (sensitivity/specificity comparable, p > 0.05), but this setting did not reflect real-world clinical decision-making which relies on metadata.

The study highlights 'epistemic uncertainty' as a risk: algorithms trained on a limited number of classes may fail or produce unreliable results for untrained or rare diseases encountered in broad clinical practice.

Data leakage risks were mitigated by using separate external validation datasets, but the authors note that hospital archives may contain biased data (e.g., unusual cases requiring biopsy) that does not represent the general population, potentially leading to false positives if not accounted for.

The algorithm's accuracy for multiclass diagnosis (Top-1 accuracy 42.6% on Dataset B) was significantly lower than clinical diagnoses (65.4%), indicating that while it can screen for malignancy, it struggles with precise differential diagnosis compared to experts.

The study used a region-based CNN (RCNN) with SENet/SE-ResNeXt-50 backbone, trained on cropped images, but tested on unprocessed clinical photographs, showing that blob detection allowed for lesion localization without pre-selection by a dermatologist.
